narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 34, your profile stands out in several ways compared to typical students in our dataset, including your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 56.6% probability that you would fail it—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision hinged on a few key factors from your academic record that carried the most weight in the prediction. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of about 5.5 and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since your 3.30 exceeds the typical 3.21 average and suggested solid prior achievement. Your family income bracket had a milder but still notable push toward failure, as upper-middle status aligned less favorably with bar passage patterns in the data compared to other groups. Overall, these elements combined to tip the scales against you, even though aspects like your LSAT and full-time status (common among 93% of students) were more neutral. Your Year 1 decile of 4 was also below the average of 5.7, contributing to the broader academic concerns. While this outcome is disappointing, it doesn't define your potential—many students improve dramatically with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the 36.7 average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor, and exploring other law schools with holistic reviews might open doors sooner. You're clearly driven, and with strategic steps, you can rewrite this story.
